1:
"And the LORD appeared unto him in the plains of Mamre: and he sat in the tent door in the heat of the day;" The witnesses note the LORD's appearing to Abraham at Mamre. On "the LORD appeared unto him in the plains of Mamre," the grammatical voices note the theophany. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "the LORD appeared unto him in the plains (oaks/terebinths) of Mamre" — the LORD appeared to Abraham at his dwelling at Mamre (13:18; 14:13); the theophany, in which the LORD (with two angels, vv.2, 22; 19:1) came to Abraham; the gracious visit of God to his servant.

On "he sat in the tent door in the heat of the day," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the setting. Henry and Gill note that Abraham "sat in the tent door in the heat of the day" — the patriarch, a tent-dweller (Hebrews 11:9), sat at his tent door in the midday heat (resting, or watching for travelers to show hospitality); the homely setting of the divine visit; the patriarch at rest when God drew near.

On the appearing, the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the condescension: the LORD's appearing to Abraham (in human form, vv.2-8, eating with him) is a remarkable condescension; God communing familiarly with his friend (James 2:23); the visit that would bring the renewed promise of Isaac (v.10) and the disclosure of Sodom's doom (v.17); the gracious nearness of God to the patriarch. The verse displays the LORD's appearing to Abraham at Mamre.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the theophany — "the LORD appeared unto him in the plains of Mamre," the LORD appearing at Abraham's dwelling (13:18, Keil, Ellicott); "he sat in the tent door in the heat of the day," the patriarch at his tent (Hebrews 11:9, Henry, Gill); the condescension of God communing with his friend (James 2:23, Calvin, grammatical-historical). 2:
"And he lift up his eyes and looked, and, lo, three men stood by him: and when he saw them, he ran to meet them from the tent door, and bowed himself toward the ground," The witnesses note the three men appearing to Abraham — the crux of their identity. On "lo, three men stood by him," interpreters expound the identity. The grammatical-historical and reformed note that three men appeared, in human form; the dominant Christian reading holds that one of the three was the LORD himself (the Angel of the LORD, a pre-incarnate appearance of the Son of God, who remains with Abraham, vv.13, 17, 22), and the other two were angels (who go on to Sodom, 19:1); the LORD with two attendant angels. The Christian Fathers saw in the three men (addressed as one LORD) a hint or foreshadowing of the Trinity (three Persons, one God) — used in the lessons for Trinity Sunday — though Ellicott cautions that we must be careful in pressing this. The Jewish commentators take the three as three angels (called "men" because clothed in human form, appearing as men, though not needing food, v.8).

On "he ran to meet them from the tent door, and bowed himself toward the ground," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the hospitality. Henry and Gill note that Abraham "ran to meet them... and bowed himself toward the ground" — the patriarch hastened to welcome the strangers and bowed in courteous, reverent greeting (the eastern custom of hospitality); the readiness and humility of Abraham in receiving the guests (entertaining angels unawares, Hebrews 13:2); the eager hospitality of the patriarch.

On the bowing, the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the reverence: Abraham's bowing was the courteous reverence of hospitality (to honored guests); and (as he came to perceive, addressing them as "my Lord," v.3) a deeper reverence to the LORD among them; the patriarch's eager, humble welcome. The verse displays the three men appearing to Abraham.

The witnesses agree the substance: three men appeared, whom Abraham eagerly welcomed and bowed before (Henry, Gill, Clarke). The three men bear a crux: the LORD himself (the Angel of the LORD, a pre-incarnate Christ) with two angels who go to Sodom (the dominant Christian reading) vs three angels (the Jewish view) vs a hint of the Trinity (the patristic reading, with caution). The verse displays the welcomed visitors, entreated to stay (v.3).

12:

On "Sarah laughed within herself," interpreters note the laughter. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note that Sarah "laughed within herself" — she laughed silently, inwardly; the laugh was, in part, of incredulity and distrust — Sarah, considering her age and the impossibility (v.11), doubted the promise; the laugh of unbelief, which the LORD reproved (vv.13-15); though some allow a measure of pleasure or surprise mixed with the doubt, the predominant sense (and the LORD's reproof) marks her laugh as the laugh of incredulity, distinct from Abraham's laugh of faith (17:17). The reformed Calvin notes that the incredulity of Sarah is not to be excused — her laugh sprang from a want of faith, weighing the promise by nature rather than by God's power.

On "After I am waxed old shall I have pleasure, my lord being old also?" the grammatical-historical and reformed note her reasoning. (Yet the apostle, Hebrews 11:11, notes that Sarah, by faith, received strength to conceive — her initial incredulity giving way to faith.)
